title: Creating and Using a Python .venv in VS Code
text:
A virtual environment helps isolate project dependencies, avoid version conflicts, and improve reproducibility across projects. In this guide, the environment is named .venv, which is a common convention and works well with Visual Studio Code.
This guide also clarifies one of the most common sources of confusion when working with Python in VS Code:

a Python interpreter is used to run regular scripts such as .py
a Jupyter kernel is used to run notebook cells in .ipynb files and other notebook-based interfaces







Key idea



For scripts, focus on the Python interpreter.
For notebooks, focus on the Jupyter kernel.
In both cases, the safest option is to use the Python installation inside your local .venv.

title: Creating and Using a Python .venv in VS Code
text:
A virtual environment helps isolate project dependencies, avoid version conflicts, and improve reproducibility across projects. In this guide, the environment is named .venv, which is a common convention and works well with Visual Studio Code.
This guide also clarifies one of the most common sources of confusion when working with Python in VS Code:

a Python interpreter is used to run regular scripts such as .py
a Jupyter kernel is used to run notebook cells in .ipynb files and other notebook-based interfaces







Key idea



For scripts, focus on the Python interpreter.
For notebooks, focus on the Jupyter kernel.
In both cases, the safest option is to use the Python installation inside your local .venv.

title: Creating and Using a Python .venv in VS Code
text:
Prerequisites
Before starting, make sure you have installed:

Python
Visual Studio Code
Quarto if you also plan to work with .qmd files
Jupyter support through packages installed inside your environment

title: Creating and Using a Python .venv in VS Code
text:
Key concepts

What is .venv?
A .venv is a local Python virtual environment stored inside your project folder. It contains its own Python executable and its own installed libraries, which helps keep one project independent from another.


Python interpreter vs. Jupyter kernel
These two concepts are related, but they are not the same.

Python interpreter
The Python interpreter is the executable that runs Python code.
Typical examples:

./.venv/bin/python on macOS/Linux
.venv\Scripts\python.exe on Windows

When you run a .py file in VS Code, the editor uses a selected Python interpreter.


Jupyter kernel
A Jupyter kernel is the execution engine used by notebook-based tools such as:

Jupyter Notebook
JupyterLab
VS Code notebooks (.ipynb)
Quarto documents with Python code cells

A Jupyter kernel is usually associated with a specific Python interpreter. In practice, you want the notebook kernel to use the Python interpreter inside your .venv.






Practical rule




For .py files, select the correct Python interpreter
For .ipynb files, select the correct Jupyter kernel
Ideally, both should point to the Python installation inside .venv

title: Creating and Using a Python .venv in VS Code
text:
Step 1. Create the virtual environment
Create the environment from the root of your project.

macOS and Linux
cd path/to/your/project
python -m venv .venv


Windows
cd path\to\your\project
python -m venv .venv
This creates a local virtual environment named .venv inside the project directory.

title: Creating and Using a Python .venv in VS Code
text:
Step 2. Activate the virtual environment
After creating the environment, activate it in the terminal.

macOS and Linux
source .venv/bin/activate


Windows
.venv\Scripts\activate
If PowerShell blocks activation, use:
Set-ExecutionPolicy -ExecutionPolicy RemoteSigned -Scope Process
.venv\Scripts\activate
Once activated, the terminal usually shows the environment name, for example:
(.venv)






Quick check



If you see (.venv) at the beginning of the terminal prompt, the environment is usually active.

title: Creating and Using a Python .venv in VS Code
text:
Step 3. Install packages inside .venv
Once .venv is active, install the packages you need inside that environment.

For notebooks and notebook execution
pip install ipykernel jupyter


For data analysis and visualization
pip install pandas altair vega_datasets


Why these packages?

ipykernel: lets Jupyter-based tools execute Python using the current environment
jupyter: provides notebook support
pandas: data manipulation
altair: declarative visualization
vega_datasets: sample datasets for Altair







Important



Run these commands after activating .venv. Otherwise, packages may be installed in the wrong Python environment.

title: Creating and Using a Python .venv in VS Code
section: Step 4. Open the project in VS Code
text:
Step 4. Open the project in VS Code
A good practice is to open VS Code from the project root:
code .
This helps VS Code detect the project structure and the local .venv.

title: Creating and Using a Python .venv in VS Code
section: Step 5. Select the Python interpreter in VS Code
text:
Step 5. Select the Python interpreter in VS Code
For regular Python scripts such as .py, select the interpreter from the local .venv.

Typical interpreter path

macOS/Linux: ./.venv/bin/python
Windows: .venv\Scripts\python.exe



In VS Code

Open the Command Palette

macOS: Cmd + Shift + P
Windows/Linux: Ctrl + Shift + P

Run Python: Select Interpreter
Choose the interpreter from .venv




Selecting the Python interpreter in VS Code








Remember



Selecting the interpreter is especially relevant for .py files. It does not automatically guarantee that a notebook will use the same environment.

title: Creating and Using a Python .venv in VS Code
section: Step 6. Use notebooks correctly in VS Code
text:
Step 6. Use notebooks correctly in VS Code
When working with notebooks (.ipynb), VS Code uses a Jupyter kernel, not just the interpreter selector.

Best case: .venv is detected automatically
Sometimes VS Code detects the environment automatically and lets you use it directly as a notebook kernel.
If that happens:

Open the notebook
Click Select Kernel
Choose the option associated with .venv




Selecting the Jupyter kernel in VS Code




If .venv is not available as a kernel
Register the environment manually as a Jupyter kernel:
python -m ipykernel install --user --name=project-viz-env --display-name="Python viz (.venv)"


Meaning of the command

python: uses the currently active interpreter, ideally the one inside .venv
-m ipykernel install: registers a Jupyter kernel
--user: installs it only for the current user
--name: internal kernel name
--display-name: visible label in VS Code or JupyterLab

After this, the kernel should appear in notebook interfaces.






Tip



Manual kernel registration is useful when VS Code does not detect .venv automatically. In many cases, automatic detection works and manual registration is not necessary.

title: Creating and Using a Python .venv in VS Code
section: Step 7. Select the Jupyter kernel in VS Code
text:
Step 7. Select the Jupyter kernel in VS Code
For a notebook:

Open the .ipynb file
Click Select Kernel
Choose the kernel associated with your .venv

If you registered it manually, it may appear with a display name such as:
Python viz (.venv)
Then the notebook will execute cells using the project environment.

title: Creating and Using a Python .venv in VS Code
section: Step 8. Verify that the notebook is using .venv
text:
Step 8. Verify that the notebook is using .venv
Inside a notebook cell, run:
import sys
print(sys.executable)
You want to see a path pointing to your local .venv, such as:
.../your-project/.venv/bin/python
If it points to a global Python instead, then the notebook is using the wrong kernel.






Why this matters



A notebook may open correctly and still use the wrong environment. Checking sys.executable is a simple way to confirm that the notebook is really running from .venv.

title: Creating and Using a Python .venv in VS Code
text:
Step 9. Deactivate the environment
When you finish working in the terminal, deactivate the environment with:
deactivate
This exits the virtual environment and returns to the default shell Python.

title: Creating and Using a Python .venv in VS Code
text:
Common mistakes

Mistake 1. Confusing interpreter and kernel
A Python interpreter and a Jupyter kernel are related, but not identical.

Scripts use an interpreter
Notebooks use a kernel
The kernel should ideally use the interpreter from .venv



Mistake 2. Installing packages outside .venv
Always activate .venv before running pip install ....


Mistake 3. Assuming VS Code always detects .venv
Often it does, but not always. Manual kernel registration is optional, not mandatory.


Mistake 4. Clicking Install for the wrong Python
If VS Code says something like “Running cells with Python X requires ipykernel”, first check whether that Python is really your .venv.

title: Creating and Using a Python .venv in VS Code
text:
Recommended workflow

Create .venv
Activate .venv
Install required packages
Open the project in VS Code
Select the interpreter for .py files
Select the Jupyter kernel for .ipynb files
Register the kernel only if automatic detection fails

title: Creating and Using a Python .venv in VS Code
text:
Final note






Good practice



Use .venv as the default environment for each project. This keeps dependencies isolated and makes both scripts and notebooks more reproducible.

title: Getting Started with Git for Project Version Control
text:
Git is a distributed version control system used to track changes in source code and collaborate efficiently. It is especially useful in programming, research, and data science projects because it preserves the history of changes, supports rollback, and improves reproducibility.
In a typical project workflow, Git helps you:

track the history of changes
collaborate more safely
recover previous versions if needed
keep experiments and project evolution organized







Why Git matters



Git is not only for software engineering. It is also highly valuable in research, data science, academic projects, and technical documentation because it improves traceability and reproducibility.

title: Getting Started with Git for Project Version Control
text:
Git is a distributed version control system used to track changes in source code and collaborate efficiently. It is especially useful in programming, research, and data science projects because it preserves the history of changes, supports rollback, and improves reproducibility.
In a typical project workflow, Git helps you:

track the history of changes
collaborate more safely
recover previous versions if needed
keep experiments and project evolution organized







Why Git matters



Git is not only for software engineering. It is also highly valuable in research, data science, academic projects, and technical documentation because it improves traceability and reproducibility.

title: Getting Started with Git for Project Version Control
text:
Prerequisites
Before starting, make sure you have:

Git installed on your computer
Visual Studio Code or another code editor
a project folder already created
a GitHub account if you want to publish the repository online

title: Getting Started with Git for Project Version Control
text:
Step 1. Create a .gitignore file
A .gitignore file tells Git which files and folders should not be tracked.
This is important for keeping the repository clean and avoiding unnecessary or machine-specific files.
Create the file from the root of your project:
touch .gitignore
Then add rules such as the following:
# Python
.venv/
__pycache__/
*.pyc

# Environment variables
.env

# macOS
.DS_Store

# Jupyter
.ipynb_checkpoints/

# Quarto
_site/
.quarto/
/.quarto/
*_files/

Why is .gitignore important?

prevents committing unnecessary or large files
avoids uploading virtual environments such as .venv
keeps the repository cleaner and lighter
improves portability across machines







Best practice



Create .gitignore before running git init, so the repository starts clean from the beginning.

title: Getting Started with Git for Project Version Control
text:
Step 2. Initialize Git locally
Once the project folder is ready and .gitignore has been created, initialize Git in the project directory.
git init
This turns your folder into a local Git repository.

Add files to staging
git add .
This stages all project files except those excluded by .gitignore.


Create the first commit
git commit -m "Initial clean commit"
This saves the first snapshot of your project history.


Check repository status
Before committing, it is a good habit to verify what Git is about to include:
git status






Recommended workflow



A simple initial workflow is:

create .gitignore
run git init
verify files with git status
stage files with git add .
create a commit with git commit

title: Getting Started with Git for Project Version Control
section: Step 3. Create the repository on GitHub
text:
Step 3. Create the repository on GitHub
To publish your project online, create a new repository on GitHub.
Recommended configuration:

Repository name: choose a meaningful name, for example project-visualization
Visibility: Public or Private
Do not initialize with README: this helps avoid unnecessary merge conflicts when linking an existing local project

After that, click Create repository.



Creating a repository on GitHub

title: Getting Started with Git for Project Version Control
section: Step 4. Link the local repository to GitHub
text:
Step 4. Link the local repository to GitHub
After creating the remote repository on GitHub, connect your local project to it.
git remote add origin https://github.com/USUARIO/proyecto-visualizacion.git
git branch -M main
git push -u origin main

Meaning of each command

git remote add origin ...: links the local repository to the remote repository on GitHub
git branch -M main: renames the default branch to main
git push -u origin main: uploads the project and sets the remote tracking branch







Replace placeholder values



Before running the command, replace USUARIO and proyecto-visualizacion with your real GitHub username and repository name.

title: Getting Started with Git for Project Version Control
text:
Common mistakes

Mistake 1. Creating .gitignore too late
If you run git init and stage files before creating .gitignore, unnecessary files may already be tracked.


Mistake 2. Uploading .venv
A virtual environment should usually stay local. Do not include .venv/ in your repository.


Mistake 3. Forgetting to check git status
Committing without reviewing the status can lead to accidental uploads of unwanted files.


Mistake 4. Initializing the GitHub repository with a README
If your local project already exists, initializing the remote repository with a README can introduce avoidable merge conflicts.

title: Getting Started with Git for Project Version Control
text:
Recommended flow
Use this sequence for a clean first setup:





flowchart TD
    A[Create project folder] --> B[Create .gitignore]
    B --> C[Run git init]
    C --> D[Check git status]
    D --> E[Run git add .]
    E --> F[Create first commit]
    F --> G[Create repository on GitHub]
    G --> H[Link remote origin]
    H --> I[Push to main]

title: Getting Started with Git for Project Version Control
text:
Final note
Git becomes much more useful when used consistently from the start of a project. Even in small academic or personal projects, it helps maintain order, traceability, and reproducibility.






Final recommendation



For projects in Python, Jupyter, or Quarto, always review your .gitignore carefully before the first commit. This small step prevents many common repository problems later.

title: Creating a Quarto Project with _quarto.yml and Output to docs/
text:
A Quarto project helps you organize multiple documents, share configuration across files, and control where rendered outputs are written. One of the most practical setups is to define a project with _quarto.yml and send rendered output to a docs/ folder.
This structure is especially useful when you want to keep source files clean while also preparing the project for publication with GitHub Pages.






Why use a Quarto project?



A Quarto project lets you:

manage multiple .qmd files in one directory
share configuration through _quarto.yml
render the whole project with one command
redirect generated output to a separate folder such as docs/

title: Creating a Quarto Project with _quarto.yml and Output to docs/
text:
A Quarto project helps you organize multiple documents, share configuration across files, and control where rendered outputs are written. One of the most practical setups is to define a project with _quarto.yml and send rendered output to a docs/ folder.
This structure is especially useful when you want to keep source files clean while also preparing the project for publication with GitHub Pages.






Why use a Quarto project?



A Quarto project lets you:

manage multiple .qmd files in one directory
share configuration through _quarto.yml
render the whole project with one command
redirect generated output to a separate folder such as docs/

title: Creating a Quarto Project with _quarto.yml and Output to docs/
text:
Prerequisites
Before starting, make sure you have installed:

Quarto
Visual Studio Code if you want to edit and preview from an IDE
Git if you plan to publish the project on GitHub
a GitHub account if you want to deploy with GitHub Pages

title: Creating a Quarto Project with _quarto.yml and Output to docs/
text:
What is _quarto.yml?
The file _quarto.yml is the main project configuration file in Quarto. It is used to define project-level settings such as:

the project type
the output directory
website configuration
shared format options
preview behavior

In practice, _quarto.yml is the file that turns a normal folder into a Quarto project.






Key idea



If you want a folder to behave as a Quarto project, _quarto.yml is the central file that controls that behavior.

title: Creating a Quarto Project with _quarto.yml and Output to docs/
text:
Why send output to docs/?
By default, Quarto website output is often written to _site/, but using docs/ can be more convenient when you want a repository layout that is easy to inspect and publish.
Sending output to docs/ helps you:

keep generated files separate from source files
avoid clutter in the project root
make GitHub Pages configuration simpler
keep the repository structure easier to understand







Common use case



If your repository will be published with GitHub Pages, docs/ is one of the simplest output directories to use.

title: Creating a Quarto Project with _quarto.yml and Output to docs/
text:
Recommended project structure
A clean project structure could look like this:
my-quarto-project/
├── _quarto.yml
├── index.qmd
├── about.qmd
├── styles.css
├── images/
│   └── ...
└── docs/

What each file or folder does

_quarto.yml: project configuration
index.qmd: home page or main entry point
about.qmd: additional content page
styles.css: optional custom styles
images/: figures and screenshots
docs/: rendered HTML output

title: Creating a Quarto Project with _quarto.yml and Output to docs/
text:
Step 1. Create the project folder
Create a new folder for the project and move into it:
mkdir my-quarto-project
cd my-quarto-project
You can use any project name you want. The important part is to keep all Quarto source files inside this folder.

title: Creating a Quarto Project with _quarto.yml and Output to docs/
text:
Step 2. Create the main files
Create the minimum files needed for the project:
touch _quarto.yml
touch index.qmd
mkdir docs
If you want an extra page:
touch about.qmd






Note



You do not always need to create docs/ manually. Quarto can create the output directory during render, but creating it explicitly helps make the project structure clear from the beginning.

title: Creating a Quarto Project with _quarto.yml and Output to docs/
text:
Step 3. Create _quarto.yml
Here is a minimal and clean configuration for a website project that renders output to docs/:
project:
  type: website
  output-dir: docs

website:
  title: "My Quarto Project"
  navbar:
    left:
      - href: index.qmd
        text: Home
      - href: about.qmd
        text: About

format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true

Explanation

project
This section defines the project itself.

type: website tells Quarto that this is a website project
output-dir: docs tells Quarto to place rendered output in docs/



website
This section defines website-specific settings.

title sets the website title
navbar creates top navigation links



format
This section defines shared output settings.

html means the project will render to HTML
theme: cosmo applies a built-in visual theme
toc: true enables a table of contents

title: Creating a Quarto Project with _quarto.yml and Output to docs/
text:
Step 4. Create index.qmd
A minimal index.qmd file could look like this:
---
title: "Home"
---

# Welcome

This is the home page of my Quarto project.

## Next steps

- Add more pages
- Customize the theme
- Publish the project
If you created an about.qmd page, you can use:
---
title: "About"
---

# About this project

This page contains additional information about the site.

title: Creating a Quarto Project with _quarto.yml and Output to docs/
text:
Step 5. Preview the project
From the project root, run:
quarto preview
Quarto will detect _quarto.yml, treat the folder as a project, render the site, and start a local preview server.

What happens during preview?

Quarto renders project files to docs/
a local server is started
the project can be viewed in a browser
changes are re-rendered while preview is active







Good to know



If your project uses output-dir: docs, the rendered files remain in docs/ after preview or render. They are not temporary files that disappear when preview stops.

title: Creating a Quarto Project with _quarto.yml and Output to docs/
text:
Step 6. Render the full project
To render the full project without launching preview, run:
quarto render
This is useful when you want to generate the final site output in docs/ without opening the local preview server.

title: Creating a Quarto Project with _quarto.yml and Output to docs/
section: Step 7. Understand what goes into docs/
text:
Step 7. Understand what goes into docs/
After rendering, the docs/ directory will contain generated output such as:

index.html
about.html
supporting assets
other files needed by the website

A possible rendered structure could look like this:
docs/
├── index.html
├── about.html
├── site_libs/
└── search.json
The exact contents may vary depending on the project configuration and features enabled.

title: Creating a Quarto Project with _quarto.yml and Output to docs/
section: Step 8. Keep source and output separate
text:
Step 8. Keep source and output separate
One of the main benefits of output-dir: docs is that source files remain in the project root while rendered output is grouped in one place.

Source files stay here

_quarto.yml
index.qmd
about.qmd
styles.css
images/



Generated output goes here

docs/index.html
docs/about.html
other generated assets

This makes it easier to distinguish between files you edit and files Quarto generates.

title: Creating a Quarto Project with _quarto.yml and Output to docs/
text:
Step 9. Publish with GitHub Pages
If your repository is hosted on GitHub, this structure works well with GitHub Pages.

Typical workflow

Push the repository to GitHub
Open the repository settings
Go to Pages
Under Source, choose the branch
Select the docs/ folder
Save the configuration

After that, GitHub Pages can publish the site from the rendered content in docs/.






Practical recommendation



A common workflow is:

edit .qmd source files
run quarto render
commit both source files and the rendered docs/ output
push to GitHub

title: Creating a Quarto Project with _quarto.yml and Output to docs/
text:
Optional improvements

Add a custom stylesheet
You can add a file such as styles.css and reference it in _quarto.yml:
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
    css: styles.css


Add preview options
You can also control preview behavior in _quarto.yml:
project:
  type: website
  output-dir: docs
  preview:
    browser: true
This is useful if you want project-specific preview settings.


Add a site URL
If the project will be published, you can define the website URL:
website:
  title: "My Quarto Project"
  site-url: "https://your-username.github.io/your-repository/"

title: Creating a Quarto Project with _quarto.yml and Output to docs/
text:
Common mistakes

Mistake 1. Forgetting _quarto.yml
Without _quarto.yml, Quarto may treat files as individual documents instead of as a project.


Mistake 2. Mixing source files with rendered output
If output is written to the project root, the directory can become harder to manage. Using docs/ keeps things more organized.


Mistake 3. Editing files inside docs/
The docs/ folder contains generated output. In most cases, you should edit the source .qmd files instead, then render again.


Mistake 4. Forgetting to render before publishing
If GitHub Pages is configured to publish from docs/, make sure the project has been rendered so that the latest HTML files are actually there.


Mistake 5. Using broken navigation links
If a page is listed in the navbar, make sure the corresponding .qmd file exists.

title: Creating a Quarto Project with _quarto.yml and Output to docs/
text:
Recommended workflow
A clean workflow for this kind of project is:

create the project folder
create _quarto.yml
define output-dir: docs
create index.qmd and other pages
preview locally with quarto preview
render the full site with quarto render
publish docs/ through GitHub Pages if needed

title: Creating a Quarto Project with _quarto.yml and Output to docs/
text:
Complete minimal example

_quarto.yml
project:
  type: website
  output-dir: docs

website:
  title: "My Quarto Project"
  navbar:
    left:
      - href: index.qmd
        text: Home
      - href: about.qmd
        text: About

format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true


index.qmd
---
title: "Home"
---

# Welcome

This is the home page of my Quarto project.


about.qmd
---
title: "About"
---

# About

This page contains extra information.

title: Creating a Quarto Project with _quarto.yml and Output to docs/
text:
Final note
Using _quarto.yml together with output-dir: docs is a simple and effective way to keep a Quarto project organized. It separates source files from generated output, supports clean project structure, and works especially well when the final site will be published with GitHub Pages.






Final recommendation



If your goal is to keep the project clean and make publishing easier, docs/ is usually a very practical output directory for Quarto website projects.

title: Home
text:
abc



a
b
c

title: Home
text:
a
b
c

title: Publishing a Quarto Project with GitHub Pages
text:
GitHub Pages is a free static hosting service provided by GitHub. It allows you to publish websites directly from a repository, which makes it especially useful for Quarto reports, project websites, and student portfolios.
In a typical workflow, you first prepare and push your project to GitHub, and then configure GitHub Pages from the repository settings so the site becomes publicly accessible.






Typical use cases



According to the source material, common uses of GitHub Pages include:

Quarto reports and websites
Student portfolios

title: Publishing a Quarto Project with GitHub Pages
text:
GitHub Pages is a free static hosting service provided by GitHub. It allows you to publish websites directly from a repository, which makes it especially useful for Quarto reports, project websites, and student portfolios.
In a typical workflow, you first prepare and push your project to GitHub, and then configure GitHub Pages from the repository settings so the site becomes publicly accessible.






Typical use cases



According to the source material, common uses of GitHub Pages include:

Quarto reports and websites
Student portfolios

title: Publishing a Quarto Project with GitHub Pages
text:
Prerequisites
Before enabling GitHub Pages, make sure you have:

a GitHub repository already created
your project pushed to GitHub
a Quarto project or other static content ready to publish
a clear output folder strategy, such as publishing from docs/







Good practice



If your project is built with Quarto, it is common to configure the rendered site so the final output is written to a publishable folder such as docs/.

title: Publishing a Quarto Project with GitHub Pages
section: Step 1. Open your repository on GitHub
text:
Step 1. Open your repository on GitHub
Go to the GitHub repository that contains the project you want to publish. The GitHub Pages configuration starts from the repository interface.

title: Publishing a Quarto Project with GitHub Pages
text:
Step 2. Open the repository settings
Inside the repository:

Click Settings
In the left sidebar, scroll down
Click Pages

These are the configuration steps indicated in the source material.



GitHub repository settings and Pages section

title: Publishing a Quarto Project with GitHub Pages
text:
Step 3. Choose the publishing source
Under Source, configure the repository publishing options.
The source material indicates the following setup:

Branch: main
or master if that is your default branch
Folder: / (docs)

Then click Save.






Important



Make sure the selected branch matches the branch you are actually using in the repository. In many projects this is main, but some repositories may still use master.

title: Publishing a Quarto Project with GitHub Pages
section: Step 4. Wait for GitHub to publish the site
text:
Step 4. Wait for GitHub to publish the site
After saving the Pages configuration, GitHub will generate a public link after a short delay. The source indicates that this usually takes a few minutes.
A typical published URL looks like this:
https://<your-username>.github.io/<repository-name>/

title: Publishing a Quarto Project with GitHub Pages
text:
How this fits with Quarto
A common workflow for Quarto projects is:

Write and render your .qmd files
Make sure the generated site is placed in the folder you plan to publish
Push the repository to GitHub
Enable GitHub Pages from the repository settings
Share the generated public URL







Suggested project logic



If you are building a small course website or technical documentation site, keeping the rendered output in docs/ can simplify publishing with GitHub Pages.

title: Publishing a Quarto Project with GitHub Pages
text:
Common mistakes

Mistake 1. Selecting the wrong branch
If GitHub Pages is configured to publish from main but your content is on another branch, the site will not publish correctly.


Mistake 2. Selecting the wrong folder
If Pages is configured to use / (docs), but the rendered site is not inside docs/, GitHub will not find the expected files.


Mistake 3. Expecting the site to appear instantly
GitHub Pages may take a few minutes to generate and expose the public link. fileciteturn1file0L17-L20


Mistake 4. Forgetting to push changes
Local changes do not affect GitHub Pages until they are committed and pushed to the repository.

title: Publishing a Quarto Project with GitHub Pages
text:
Recommended workflow
To keep the process simple and reproducible:

Keep your project under Git version control
Push the latest version to GitHub
Configure GitHub Pages from Settings > Pages
Publish from the correct branch and folder
Test the public URL after deployment

title: Publishing a Quarto Project with GitHub Pages
text:
Final note
GitHub Pages is a simple and effective way to publish static academic and technical content for free. For Quarto users, it is especially useful for sharing reports, course materials, and small websites directly from a GitHub repository.

title: Installing and Verifying Quarto in VS Code
text:
Quarto is an open-source publishing system for technical and scientific communication. It allows you to combine narrative text, executable code, and outputs such as figures, tables, and interactive content in a single reproducible document.
A Quarto document typically uses the .qmd extension and can be rendered to multiple formats, including HTML, PDF, slides, dashboards, and books.






Why Quarto is useful



Quarto is especially useful when you want to keep text, code, and results together in one place and generate polished outputs from the same source file.

title: Installing and Verifying Quarto in VS Code
text:
Quarto is an open-source publishing system for technical and scientific communication. It allows you to combine narrative text, executable code, and outputs such as figures, tables, and interactive content in a single reproducible document.
A Quarto document typically uses the .qmd extension and can be rendered to multiple formats, including HTML, PDF, slides, dashboards, and books.






Why Quarto is useful



Quarto is especially useful when you want to keep text, code, and results together in one place and generate polished outputs from the same source file.

title: Installing and Verifying Quarto in VS Code
text:
What Quarto is used for
Quarto can be used to create:

HTML pages and websites
PDF reports
slide presentations
dashboards
books
technical documentation
reproducible analysis reports

It supports narrative writing in Markdown together with code in languages such as Python, R, and Julia.

title: Installing and Verifying Quarto in VS Code
text:
Prerequisites
Before starting, make sure you have:

Visual Studio Code installed
permission to install software on your computer
access to a terminal

If you plan to execute Python code inside Quarto documents, you should also have Python installed.

title: Installing and Verifying Quarto in VS Code
text:
Step 1. Install Quarto
To install Quarto:

Go to the official Quarto website.
Open the installation or getting started section.
Select your operating system.
Download the installer.
Run the installer and complete the setup process.

After installation, verify it from the terminal with:
quarto check
quarto --version
If Quarto was installed correctly, these commands should display the installed version and system configuration.






Tip



Use both commands after installation:

quarto --version confirms that Quarto is available
quarto check helps detect configuration problems

title: Installing and Verifying Quarto in VS Code
section: Step 2. Install the Quarto extension in VS Code
text:
Step 2. Install the Quarto extension in VS Code
Once Quarto is installed, enable support in VS Code by installing the Quarto extension.

In VS Code

Open VS Code
Open the Extensions panel
Search for Quarto
Install the extension published by the Quarto Dev Team
Restart VS Code if needed

This extension provides features such as:

syntax highlighting for .qmd files
preview and render integration
editor support for Quarto projects




Install the Quarto extension from the VS Code Extensions panel

title: Installing and Verifying Quarto in VS Code
section: Step 3. Verify that Quarto works in VS Code
text:
Step 3. Verify that Quarto works in VS Code
After installing the extension:

Open a Quarto document with extension .qmd
Check whether a Render or Preview button appears in the editor
Try previewing or rendering the document directly from VS Code

If those features are available, then Quarto is correctly integrated with VS Code.






Important



If Quarto works in the terminal but not in VS Code, the problem is often related to the editor integration, the extension state, or the current file type.

title: Installing and Verifying Quarto in VS Code
section: Step 4. Create a minimal test document
text:
Step 4. Create a minimal test document
A simple test file can be:
---
title: "My first Quarto document"
format: html
---

# Hello Quarto

This is my first Quarto page.
Save the file as:
test.qmd
Then render it using one of these options:

the Render button in VS Code
the Preview button in VS Code, if available
the terminal with:

quarto render test.qmd

title: Installing and Verifying Quarto in VS Code
text:
Step 5. Understand render vs. preview
These two actions are related, but they are not the same.

Render
quarto render generates the output file, for example an HTML page.


Preview
quarto preview starts a local preview server and lets you inspect changes while you edit the document.
Example:
quarto preview test.qmd






Key idea



Use render when you want the final output.
Use preview when you want to edit and inspect changes interactively.

title: Installing and Verifying Quarto in VS Code
text:
Recommended verification checklist
Before starting to work seriously with Quarto, confirm all of the following:

quarto --version works in the terminal
quarto check runs without critical errors
the Quarto extension is installed in VS Code
.qmd files open correctly in the editor
the Render or Preview button appears
a simple .qmd file renders successfully

title: Installing and Verifying Quarto in VS Code
text:
Common issues

Quarto command not found
If the terminal says quarto: command not found, Quarto is probably not installed correctly or is not available in your system PATH.


VS Code does not show Render or Preview
Possible causes include:

the Quarto extension is not installed
VS Code needs to be restarted
the file is not saved with extension .qmd
the current workspace has not refreshed correctly



Quarto is installed but preview does not work
Check the following:

the file has valid YAML at the top
the Quarto extension is enabled
the document does not contain syntax errors
Quarto works correctly from the terminal







Common mistake



Do not assume that Quarto is broken just because preview fails inside VS Code. Always verify first whether quarto render or quarto preview works from the terminal.

title: Installing and Verifying Quarto in VS Code
text:
Recommended workflow

Install Quarto
Verify the installation in the terminal
Install the Quarto extension in VS Code
Open a .qmd file
Check for Render or Preview in the editor
Create and test a minimal Quarto document
Use quarto render or quarto preview as needed

title: Installing and Verifying Quarto in VS Code
text:
Final note
Quarto becomes especially useful when combined with reproducible workflows in VS Code, because it allows you to keep code, text, and outputs together in a single document.
As your projects grow, you can extend the same workflow to websites, reports, presentations, and course materials built from .qmd files.
